pattern: Temporal bone trauma with mastoid ﬂ uid

findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a radiolucent fracture line oriented parallel to the long axis of the petrous temporal bone, extending from the squamous portion into the mastoid air cells. This fracture pattern typically bypasses the dense bone of the otic capsule.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include the presence of fluid or blood products within the mastoid air cells, middle ear cavity, or external auditory canal. There may also be associated ossicular chain disruption, most commonly incudostapedial joint subluxation, classically presenting as the 'ice cream falling off the cone' on coronal reconstructions.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of the fracture line crossing perpendicular to the petrous apex to involve the otic capsule or the internal auditory canal. Furthermore, there is no disruption of the carotid canal or jugular foramen to suggest major vascular injury.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the longitudinal orientation of the fracture line through the mastoid and middle ear with sparing of the inner ear structures.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Longitudinal temporal bone fracture, given the specific fracture trajectory along the long axis of the temporal bone extending into the mastoid portion. This is the most common subtype of temporal bone fracture and classically results in conductive hearing loss due to hemotympanum, tympanic membrane rupture, or ossicular dislocation.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Transverse temporal bone fracture, however this typically runs perpendicular to the long axis of the petrous pyramid, frequently traversing the otic capsule or internal auditory canal. Consequently, transverse fractures more commonly result in profound sensorineural hearing loss, vertigo, and a higher incidence of facial nerve palsy, which do not align with the classic longitudinal pattern seen here.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Mixed temporal bone fracture, given that combinations of longitudinal and transverse patterns can occur and may involve critical vascular channels such as the petrous internal carotid artery or jugular bulb. Missing a mixed fracture complex could lead to catastrophic delayed complications like massive hemorrhage, cerebrospinal fluid leak, or irreversible neurosensory deficits.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to meticulously trace the fracture line to assess for involvement of the facial nerve canal and to identify any ossicular chain dislocation.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated high-resolution CT of the temporal bones if only a standard trauma CT head has been performed, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely delineating the complex microanatomy of the middle and inner ear, the facial nerve canal, and the ossicles.
management.ctPhases: I would specify thin-slice non-contrast acquisitions with a high-spatial-frequency bone algorithm and multiplanar reformats in both the axial and coronal planes to optimally assess the tegmen tympani, facial nerve course, and ossicular alignment.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the ENT and Trauma Surgery MDT. My report would clearly document the precise trajectory of the fracture, the integrity of the ossicular chain, and any involvement of the facial nerve canal or tegmen tympani to guide conservative management or surgical repair.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a radiolucent fracture line oriented parallel to the long axis of the petrous temporal bone, extending from the squamous portion into the mastoid air cells. This fracture pattern typically bypasses the dense bone of the otic capsule. Supporting features include the presence of fluid or blood products within the mastoid air cells, middle ear cavity, or external auditory canal. There may also be associated ossicular chain disruption, most commonly incudostapedial joint subluxation, classically presenting as the 'ice cream falling off the cone' on coronal reconstructions. Importantly, there is no evidence of the fracture line crossing perpendicular to the petrous apex to involve the otic capsule or the internal auditory canal. Furthermore, there is no disruption of the carotid canal or jugular foramen to suggest major vascular injury. The feature that most influences my thinking is the longitudinal orientation of the fracture line through the mastoid and middle ear with sparing of the inner ear structures.

The appearances are most in keeping with Longitudinal temporal bone fracture, given the specific fracture trajectory along the long axis of the temporal bone extending into the mastoid portion. This is the most common subtype of temporal bone fracture and classically results in conductive hearing loss due to hemotympanum, tympanic membrane rupture, or ossicular dislocation. The principle differential would be Transverse temporal bone fracture, however this typically runs perpendicular to the long axis of the petrous pyramid, frequently traversing the otic capsule or internal auditory canal. Consequently, transverse fractures more commonly result in profound sensorineural hearing loss, vertigo, and a higher incidence of facial nerve palsy, which do not align with the classic longitudinal pattern seen here. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Mixed temporal bone fracture, given that combinations of longitudinal and transverse patterns can occur and may involve critical vascular channels such as the petrous internal carotid artery or jugular bulb. Missing a mixed fracture complex could lead to catastrophic delayed complications like massive hemorrhage, cerebrospinal fluid leak, or irreversible neurosensory deficits.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to meticulously trace the fracture line to assess for involvement of the facial nerve canal and to identify any ossicular chain dislocation. I would recommend a dedicated high-resolution CT of the temporal bones if only a standard trauma CT head has been performed, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely delineating the complex microanatomy of the middle and inner ear, the facial nerve canal, and the ossicles. I would specify thin-slice non-contrast acquisitions with a high-spatial-frequency bone algorithm and multiplanar reformats in both the axial and coronal planes to optimally assess the tegmen tympani, facial nerve course, and ossicular alignment. This should go to the ENT and Trauma Surgery MDT. My report would clearly document the precise trajectory of the fracture, the integrity of the ossicular chain, and any involvement of the facial nerve canal or tegmen tympani to guide conservative management or surgical repair.
